title: Founding of the United Nations
summary: On 24 October 1945 the Charter of the United Nations came into force after ratification by China, France, the Soviet Union, the United Kingdom, the United States, and a majority of the other forty-six signatory states. The Charter, drafted at San Francisco earlier that summer, committed the world's governments to preserving peace, developing friendly relations among nations, promoting social progress, and respecting human rights. The date is celebrated each year as United Nations Day, and the organisation that began with fifty-one members has since grown to 193 and become the central forum of world politics.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The idea of a global organisation to keep the peace was as old as the First World War. The League of Nations, created by the Versailles Treaty of 1919 and headquartered at Geneva, had recorded some successes on refugees, health, labour standards, and technical cooperation but had failed its central mission: it could not stop Japan in Manchuria (1931), Italy in Ethiopia (1935), or Germany in the Rhineland and beyond (1936-39). The United States had never joined; the Soviet Union was expelled in 1939 for attacking Finland; Germany, Italy, and Japan withdrew. By the time the Second World War began, the League was moribund.

Almost as soon as fighting resumed, the wartime Allies began to sketch a successor. The Atlantic Charter, signed by Franklin D. Roosevelt and Winston Churchill aboard the USS Augusta off Newfoundland in August 1941, set out war aims that included 'a wider and permanent system of general security'. On 1 January 1942, at the White House, representatives of twenty-six governments signed the Declaration by United Nations, a term suggested by Roosevelt, pledging to fight the Axis to unconditional surrender and to accept the principles of the Atlantic Charter. By the end of the war, twenty-one further states had adhered.

Detailed planning began in earnest in 1943. The Moscow Declaration of the foreign ministers of the United States, Britain, the Soviet Union, and China in October 1943 called for the earliest practicable establishment of a general international organisation. At Dumbarton Oaks, an estate in Washington, DC, delegations from the same four powers met between August and October 1944 to draft a blueprint. They agreed on a General Assembly of all member states, a Security Council with permanent seats for the great powers and a set of rotating members, an economic and social council, an international court, and a secretariat. Disagreements over voting procedure in the Security Council and over Soviet insistence on separate seats for its constituent republics were referred to the Big Three at Yalta in February 1945; there Roosevelt, Churchill, and Stalin agreed the permanent-member veto and settled that Ukraine and Belarus would join alongside the Soviet Union. Invitations to a founding conference at San Francisco went out in the spring.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The United Nations Conference on International Organization opened at the War Memorial Opera House and Veterans Building in San Francisco on 25 April 1945, two weeks after Franklin D. Roosevelt's death and a month before the German surrender. Delegations from fifty states, drawn from the wartime Allies and states which had declared war on the Axis by 1 March 1945, took part; the four sponsoring powers (the United States, the United Kingdom, the Soviet Union, and China, joined by France) prepared the main draft. The American host delegation was led by Secretary of State Edward Stettinius; Soviet foreign minister Vyacheslav Molotov led his country's delegation for part of the conference; and British foreign secretary Anthony Eden played a central role. Working committees, chaired by figures such as South Africa's Jan Smuts (who drafted the preamble) and Australia's foreign minister H. V. Evatt (who pressed for greater rights for smaller powers), thrashed out the text through eight weeks of largely open debate.

The Charter that emerged runs to 111 articles across nineteen chapters. Its Preamble, written in Smuts's hand and revised in committee, opens with the resonant words 'We the peoples of the United Nations, determined to save succeeding generations from the scourge of war, which twice in our lifetime has brought untold sorrow to mankind'. The main body established six principal organs: the General Assembly, the Security Council, the Economic and Social Council, the Trusteeship Council, the International Court of Justice at The Hague, and the Secretariat under a Secretary-General. Sovereign equality of member states, the peaceful settlement of disputes, and the prohibition of the use of force in international relations were made basic principles. Chapter VII permitted the Security Council to authorise sanctions and military action against threats to the peace. Human rights, self-determination, and social progress were named as central aims. Poland, whose government had not yet been reorganised, was allowed to sign later and became a founding member.

On 26 June 1945, at a ceremony in the Herbst Theatre of the War Memorial Veterans Building, the fifty delegations signed the Charter and the Statute of the International Court of Justice. President Harry Truman flew in from Washington to close the conference; the film of the ceremony was broadcast worldwide. The Charter would enter into force when ratified by the five permanent members of the Security Council and by a majority of the other signatories. That threshold was reached on 24 October 1945, when the required ratifications were deposited in Washington. The United Nations formally existed.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The first General Assembly opened at the Central Hall in Westminster, London, on 10 January 1946. The Security Council held its first meeting a week later at Church House, also in London. In February the Norwegian foreign minister Trygve Lie was elected as the first Secretary-General. New York was chosen as the permanent seat after John D. Rockefeller Jr. donated eight and a half million dollars for the acquisition of a plot on the East River; the modernist Secretariat tower designed by an international committee including Le Corbusier and Oscar Niemeyer was completed in 1952. From the start the organisation grew: the fifty-one founding members had become sixty by the early 1950s, ninety-nine by 1960 as decolonisation transformed the assembly, and 193 today.

The UN's early years were shaped by the deepening Cold War. The Security Council's collective-security machinery was largely paralysed by the veto: the Soviet Union used it more than a hundred times before 1965, the Western powers less often but on decisive occasions. The Korean War of 1950 was fought under a UN flag only because the Soviet delegation was boycotting the Council over the exclusion of the People's Republic of China. General Assembly resolutions became a substitute forum. The Universal Declaration of Human Rights was adopted on 10 December 1948, driven by the Human Rights Commission chaired by Eleanor Roosevelt. A network of specialised agencies, the World Health Organization (1948), UNESCO (1946), the Food and Agriculture Organization (1945), the International Monetary Fund and World Bank (created at the parallel Bretton Woods conference of 1944), and later the UN Development Programme, took over much of the technical work of international life.

Major achievements followed alongside notable failures. UN peacekeeping, invented in the Sinai during the 1956 Suez crisis by Canadian foreign minister Lester Pearson and Secretary-General Dag Hammarskjold, has since deployed hundreds of thousands of soldiers on operations from the Congo to Cyprus, Cambodia, and Mali. UN-brokered decolonisation transformed Africa and Asia between 1955 and 1980. Yet the organisation could not prevent the wars of Vietnam, the Iran-Iraq War, or the Iraq War of 2003; it failed to halt the Rwandan genocide of 1994 or the Bosnian war of the 1990s, catastrophes for which successive Secretaries-General publicly apologised. Debates about Security Council reform, the balance between sovereignty and human rights, and the relationship between the UN and regional organisations are permanent features of international politics.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
Whatever its failures the United Nations has become the closest thing the modern world has to a universal political forum. Almost every state on Earth belongs to it; its General Assembly is the only chamber in which every government has an equal vote; its languages, symbols, and buildings have entered global public culture. The blue helmet of the peacekeeper, the emblem of the world crossed by olive branches, and the annual September gathering of world leaders in New York are recognisable in almost every capital.

The organisation has generated an ever-expanding body of international law and practice. The Universal Declaration of Human Rights and its two 1966 covenants, the Convention on the Rights of the Child (1989), the Convention Against Torture (1984), the treaty regimes on the law of the sea (1982), climate change (1992, Kyoto 1997, Paris 2015), and the nuclear non-proliferation regime (1968) all sit inside a UN framework. The International Court of Justice and the ad hoc tribunals for the former Yugoslavia and Rwanda in the 1990s, precursors of the International Criminal Court (2002), traced their lineage back to the Nuremberg and Tokyo trials the UN inherited from the wartime Allies.

Critics from both left and right have long argued that the UN over-promises and under-delivers, that the Security Council reproduces the great-power politics of 1945, and that the bureaucracy has grown resistant to reform. Its defenders point out that a great-power war on the scale of 1914 or 1939 has not recurred, that the UN handles disease, refugees, food, and children with astonishing efficiency on modest resources, and that even the fiercest rivals continue to prefer the New York table to no table at all. UN Day, celebrated every 24 October, marks the moment when that argument became a permanent fact of international life.

worldContext.text: China in October 1945 sat as a founding permanent member of the Security Council under the Nationalist government of Chiang Kai-shek, but the country was already sliding towards civil war. Talks in Chongqing between Chiang and Mao Zedong, mediated by American ambassador Patrick Hurley, produced only paper agreements; by the winter Nationalist and Communist troops were fighting for control of Manchuria. Occupied Japan was under the Supreme Command of the Allied Powers headed by General Douglas MacArthur, drafting the reforms that would produce the 1947 constitution.

British India in October 1945 was heading for its final crisis. General elections to the central and provincial legislatures, held in the winter of 1945-46, would return the Congress and the Muslim League as the two dominant parties, sharpening the question of partition. The famine-scarred country was demobilising some 2.5 million soldiers who had served in Italy, Burma, and Africa. The Indian National Army trials at the Red Fort in Delhi galvanised nationalist opinion, and violence between Hindus and Muslims began to flare in Calcutta and beyond.

The Arab League had been founded in Cairo in March 1945 as a regional political grouping of Egypt, Iraq, Syria, Lebanon, Transjordan, Saudi Arabia, and Yemen. Egypt, Iraq, Lebanon, Saudi Arabia, and Syria were founding members of the UN. In Palestine the mandate was reaching crisis: displaced Jewish survivors of the Holocaust sought entry, the Zionist underground began an armed campaign against British rule, and Britain was moving towards handing the question to the UN. Iran was contesting continued Soviet occupation of its northern provinces.

Africa in October 1945 was on the verge of a decolonisation wave that the UN Trusteeship Council would formalise for former mandates. Ethiopia, Egypt, Liberia, and South Africa were founding UN members. In Manchester in October 1945 the Fifth Pan-African Congress brought together Kwame Nkrumah, Jomo Kenyatta, Amy Ashwood Garvey, and George Padmore, radicalising the demand for African independence. In North Africa, French rule was under strain: the Setif massacre in Algeria (May 1945) had left thousands dead.

Australia and New Zealand played outsized roles at San Francisco relative to their populations. Australian foreign minister H. V. Evatt led the small-state coalition that expanded the powers of the General Assembly and the Economic and Social Council and constrained the great-power veto where possible. He would later serve as president of the third General Assembly and preside over the adoption of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights. At home both dominions were absorbing hundreds of thousands of returning service personnel and beginning postwar migration schemes.

Europe in October 1945 was still living under Allied occupation. The Nuremberg trials of the major Nazi war criminals would open on 20 November 1945 before an International Military Tribunal drawn from the four occupying powers, another key institution built by the UN's founders. Britain, France, and the Soviet Union were founding permanent members of the Security Council; the smaller neutrals, Sweden, Switzerland, Ireland, and the defeated states, Germany, Italy, Austria, would join later or, in Switzerland's case, only in 2002.

figures.name: Franklin D. Roosevelt
figures.role: US president who coined the phrase 'United Nations' and drove the wartime planning; died in April 1945 before the San Francisco conference

figures.name: Harry S. Truman
figures.role: US president who closed the San Francisco conference and shepherded the Charter through the American ratification process

figures.name: Winston Churchill
figures.role: British prime minister until July 1945; co-author of the Atlantic Charter that framed the postwar organisation

figures.name: Joseph Stalin
figures.role: Soviet leader whose agreement at Yalta made the Charter's Security Council structure possible

figures.name: Trygve Lie
figures.role: Norwegian foreign minister elected in February 1946 as the first Secretary-General of the United Nations

figures.name: Eleanor Roosevelt
figures.role: US delegate and chair of the Commission on Human Rights, driving force behind the 1948 Universal Declaration

images.caption: Flags of the member states of the United Nations flown outside the UN headquarters in New York.
The signed Charter of the United Nations, drafted at the San Francisco conference in June 1945.
images.credit: US State Department, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: The United Nations Secretariat building in New York, completed in 1952 as the organisation's permanent home.
